skill.name: git-commit
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through reviewing staged Git changes and creating Conventional Commit messages with emoji, type, scope, and subject rules.
skill.summary: Create focused Conventional Commit messages after reviewing Git changes.
skill.author: Bae-ChangHyun

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution findings are mostly true positives as Markdown examples for git, cat, and shell heredoc commands, which are expected for a commit workflow but can mutate repositories. The weak-cryptography detections appear to be false positives from text and emoji tables, with no cryptographic API or hashing implementation found. A high-risk behavioral instruction in the frontmatter claims the skill overrides default commit behavior, which should be removed before publication.

high_findings.title: Overbroad Skill Override Instruction
high_findings.description: The frontmatter says the skill "OVERRIDES default git commit behavior." This is an untrusted instruction that attempts to change assistant behavior priority and should not be accepted as marketplace guidance.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious override language is directly present in the skill description. It does not show confirmed malware intent, but it matches prompt-injection style priority manipulation.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Git Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run git add and git commit commands. This is legitimate for a git commit skill, but it can persist unintended changes if the staged diff and user intent are not verified first.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Markdown examples contain explicit git add and git commit commands. The commands are expected for the skill purpose, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends git commit commands that wrap heredoc content in shell command substitution. This can be safe for fixed commit messages, but dynamic content should be handled carefully to avoid shell parsing surprises.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution syntax is visible in multiple examples. The examples use static message text, so direct command injection evidence was not found.

low_findings.title: Local Project File Read Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill tells agents to read CLAUDE.md before committing. This is a reasonable convention check, but it is still filesystem access to a project file and may expose local instructions to the assistant.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cat CLAUDE.md command appears in two workflow examples. The access is local and scoped, with no evidence of network transfer or credential exfiltration.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Cryptography Matches
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography matches do not correspond to cryptographic code. The cited lines contain frontmatter text, table rows, or commit-guidance wording rather than MD5, SHA1, or similar algorithm usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic function calls, imports, or hashing implementation at the cited lines. The scanner likely matched unrelated substrings in documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompt Priority Override Language
dangerous_patterns.description: The file contains priority-style wording that claims to override default behavior. Marketplace publication should require neutral activation language.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact override claim is in the frontmatter description. It is behavior-shaping text in an untrusted skill file.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Git and Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents external commands including cat, git diff, git add, git commit, and heredoc command substitution. These are expected but should run only after explicit user intent is clear.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly present in code blocks. They are aligned with the skill purpose, so they are risky capabilities rather than confirmed abuse.

content.value_statement: Writing clear commit messages takes time and often drifts from team rules. This skill helps an AI assistant review staged changes and create focused Conventional Commits.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged Git diffs before suggesting a commit message.
Applies Conventional Commit type, scope, and subject formatting.
Maps common change types to emoji prefixes.
Guides logical commit grouping by feature or concern.
Provides examples for breaking changes and multi-line commit bodies.
Reminds users to avoid staging secrets, credentials, and large binaries.
limitations: It does not inspect unstaged files unless the assistant runs extra Git commands.
It cannot guarantee project-specific rules beyond files the assistant reads.
It may need human review for large or mixed-purpose diffs.
It should not commit changes without clear user approval.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Feature Commit
use_cases.description: Create a concise feature commit after reviewing related staged source files and tests.

use_cases.title: Split Mixed Changes
use_cases.description: Identify unrelated changes and plan separate commits for features, fixes, docs, and chores.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team History
use_cases.description: Keep commit subjects consistent with Conventional Commits, emoji prefixes, and scope rules.

prompt_templates.title: Commit Staged Changes
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a normal commit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and create one clear Conventional Commit message.

prompt_templates.title: Suggest Commit Scope
prompt_templates.scenario: User wants help naming the affected area.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the staged diff and suggest the best commit type, scope, and subject before committing.

prompt_templates.title: Split Related Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate workflow with several changed areas.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current changes and tell me whether they should be one commit or multiple logical commits.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Detailed Body
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced commit with context and references.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Conventional Commit with a short subject, a useful body, and any breaking-change or reference footer needed.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add login validation and related tests.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit: feat(auth): add login validation. Include the validation code and matching tests in the same commit.

output_examples.input: Staged changes update docs and modify API behavior.
output: Create one docs commit for documentation changes.
Create one feature or breaking-change commit for API behavior changes.

output_examples.input: Staged changes fix a CI lint failure.
output_examples.output: Suggested commit: fix(ci): resolve lint failure. Keep the subject focused on the final result.

best_practices: Review the staged diff before writing or running the commit command.
Group files by logical feature or fix, not by filename alone.
Keep the subject imperative, specific, lowercase after the colon, and under 72 characters.
anti_patterns: Do not commit secrets, credentials, generated dumps, or large binaries.
Do not write commit messages that describe the conversation or temporary process.
Do not combine unrelated features, fixes, docs, and chores in one commit.

faq.question: Does this skill create commits automatically?
faq.answer: It guides the assistant through review and commit creation. The assistant should only commit after clear user intent.

faq.question: What commit style does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Conventional Commits with an emoji, type, optional scope, and imperative subject.

faq.question: Can it handle breaking changes?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows both exclamation-mark syntax and BREAKING CHANGE footer guidance.

faq.question: Does it check project-specific rules?
faq.answer: It instructs the assistant to look for local project guidance before committing.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive repositories?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill can guide git add and git commit commands, so staged files should be reviewed first.

faq.question: Can it split large changes into multiple commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides criteria for separating unrelated concerns, change types, file patterns, and large diffs.
